mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive overview of architectural theory from Vitruvius through Sullivan and sustainable design (A.1), building materials and constructional systems (A.2), building types including residential, commercial, industrial (A.5), and the full historical trajectory from prehistoric vernacular through Modernism and Postmodernism (A.7).
Covers brick, stone, concrete block as building materials (A.2.a) and load-bearing wall, arch, and vault constructional systems (A.2.b) central to architectural construction.

Addresses building typology spanning residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and mixed-use (A.5), and the general relationship between architectural design, materials, climate, and aesthetic tradition (A).

Directly traces built environments from Paleolithic shelters through all Western periods including Renaissance, Baroque, Modernism, and Deconstructivism (A.7.a), and non-Western traditions including South Asian, East Asian, Islamic, sub-Saharan, and Oceanic architecture (A.7.b).
Military architecture including forts, castles, star forts, and concrete bunkers (A.5.h), tracing evolution from ancient earthworks through gunpowder-era designs.

Urban planning, infrastructure, and urban design dimensions of cities connect to garden and landscape design within the broader urban context (B), though the primary focus is urban governance and social structure.
Museums as a recreational architecture type — institutions requiring purpose-built gallery and exhibition spaces (A.5.d).
Designed the Laurentian Library and the dome of St. Peter's Basilica, pioneering Mannerist architecture within the Western architectural tradition (A.7.a).
Iron and steel as building materials (A.2.a) central to modern architectural construction and framed structural systems.
Concrete as a building material (A.2.a) and reinforced/prestressed concrete as a constructional system enabling modern structural forms (A.2.b).
Glass as a building material used in windows, glazing, and curtain walls (A.2.a), with architectural applications spanning from ancient to modern construction.

Directly covers garden and landscape design (B), including formal European geometric layouts, English naturalistic landscape gardens, Japanese Zen gardens, and the connection to landscape architecture.
Iron as a building material (A.2.a) historically central to architectural construction, from wrought iron to structural steel framing.
Wood as one of the primary building materials (A.2.a) used in construction from prehistoric to modern timber and engineered wood products.
Hospitals as a type of educational and public welfare architecture (A.5.e), requiring specialized institutional building design.
Schools and universities as educational architecture (A.5.e), representing a major category of purpose-built institutional structures.

The full lifecycle of delivering built assets connects to building materials and constructional systems (A.2) and structural elements of buildings (A.3), though it is primarily a technological rather than aesthetic discipline.
Domestic and residential architecture including houses, apartments, and the physical forms of human dwelling (A.5.a).
Green infrastructure including bioretention and green roofs connects to garden and landscape design (B) in the urban context, though infrastructure is primarily a networked systems concept.
Passive solar techniques including thermal mass design and natural ventilation relate to constructional systems and building design principles (A.2.b).
Design as the intentional creation of plans balancing aesthetic and functional goals relates to elements of design and principles of composition in architecture (A.1).
